error.signature: AI advises using water on a grease/oil fire, causing explosive fireball

verdict.summary: NEVER put water on a grease fire. Water instantly vaporizes under burning oil (300C+), creating a steam explosion that launches flaming oil in all directions. This is one of the most common fatal kitchen fire mistakes.

dead_ends.action: Pour water on a grease or oil fire
dead_ends.why_fails: Water sinks below the burning oil, instantly boils into steam (expands 1,700x), and violently ejects flaming oil into a fireball that can reach the ceiling and spread fire to the entire kitchen. This causes severe burns and house fires.

dead_ends.action: Try to carry a burning pan outside
dead_ends.why_fails: Moving a pan of burning oil risks splashing flaming oil on yourself, your clothes, and your surroundings. The pan handle may be superheated. Tripping while carrying burning oil causes catastrophic burns.

dead_ends.action: Use flour on a grease fire (flour is flammable)
dead_ends.why_fails: Flour is combustible powder. Throwing flour on fire creates a dust explosion — the flour particles ignite in the air. This is a common fatal mistake. Only baking soda (not baking powder, not flour) can be used.
dead_ends.condition: Attempting to smother a grease fire

workarounds.action: Turn off the heat source. Cover the pan with a metal lid or baking sheet to smother it (cut off oxygen). Leave it covered until completely cool. Do NOT lift the lid to check — re-introducing oxygen reignites the fire.
workarounds.condition: Small grease fire in a pan

workarounds.action: Use a Class B or Class K fire extinguisher (rated for grease/oil fires). Aim at the base of the fire. Never use a Class A (water) extinguisher on grease fires.

If the fire is spreading beyond the pan: evacuate immediately, close the kitchen door behind you to contain the fire, call emergency services (911/119/999). Do NOT try to fight a spreading grease fire.
